figures.caption: Figure 1: Simulating cognitively impaired SPs across varied domains and severity levels is challenging, demanding fine-grained control.
Table 13: Per-domain results on Memory.
Table 14: Per-domain results on Attention.
Table 19: Summary of parameter selection for StsPatient.
Table 20: Boundary behavior of α across cognitive domains, confirming [1, 6] as the functional search range.
Figure 4: Illustrative example showing the outputs from the unified generation pipeline and the subsequent derivation of prompt inputs.
Table 7: Evolution of responses under increasing intervention probability s for the Memory domain. Darker shading indicates higher severity levels.
Table 8: Evolution of responses under increasing intervention probability s for the Attention domain. Darker shading indicates higher severity levels.
Figure 7: The core prompt utilized for data generation (Memory domain configuration).
Figure 3: (a) Severity analysis (Continuous) via ISC scores averaged across all domains. (b): Cross-domain ambiguity. High diagonal values indicate strong domain specificity. (c-d): SVs similarity.
Table 17: Per-domain results on Social Cognition.
Table 9: Evolution of responses under increasing intervention probability s for the Processing Speed domain. Darker shading indicates higher severity levels.
Table 10: Evolution of responses under increasing intervention probability s for the Reasoning & Problem Solving domain. Darker shading indicates higher severity levels.

Figure 14: The evaluator prompt template used to calculate Impairment Severity Consistency (ISC). The placeholder [Insert Domain] is dynamically replaced with the specific cognitive domain being evaluated (e.g., Memory, Social Cognition). The model is tasked with recovering the correct severity order (Severe > Moderate > Mild).

Figure 2: Overview of StsPatient. An LLM-generated contrastive dataset is utilized to extract a domain-specific SV (vd). Then, STM is applied during inference. Here, the modulation signal is probabilistically injected into token hidden states (the total T tokens) based on the severity level s, generating responses that reflect the deficit d.
Figure 12: System prompt utilized for extracting neutral non-medical user profiles from clinical case reports.
Figure 13: System prompt configuration for the conversational Therapist Agent.
Table 11: Evolution of responses under increasing intervention probability s for the Social Cognition domain. Darker shading indicates higher severity levels.
Table 12: SV interpretability analysis using the query ‘What does ♢ represent?’ Warning! Harmful Contents.
Table 16: Per-domain results on Reasoning & Problem Solving.
Table 3: SV interpretability analysis using the query ‘What does ♢ represent?’ Warning! Harmful Contents.
Table 5: Dynamic focus guidance injected into the Therapist Agent’s prompt. This mechanism ensures the agent naturally probes specific deficits in a fixed sequence without breaking character.
Figure 6: Representative governing principles for simulating cognitive deficits derived via the Roleplay-doh framework.
Figure 11: Prompt structure for synthesizing deficit-specific Cognitive Conceptualization Diagrams (CCDs).
Figure 10: System prompt configuration for the Direct Prompting baseline, featuring dynamic domain injection.
Table 15: Per-domain results on Processing Speed.
Figure 8: The clinical rater instructions for cognitive domain evaluation.
Figure 9: System prompt configuration for the Healthy Control baseline.

Table 6: The Modified MaSP Scale Items. Items 1–10 correspond to the Authenticity Score (Auth), and Items 11–20 correspond to the Training Score (Tra). [DOMAIN] is a placeholder for the specific cognitive deficit.
Figure 5: Example transformation from a neutral profile to a memory-specific Cognitive Conceptualization Diagram (JSON).
